title: Chapter 38
text: Chapter Thirty - Five “G et that AMR up here, now !” Maul shouted . In Ronin’s periphery, a soldier scrambled forward with a long, black rifle — an anti-materiel rifle, Ronin’s data said. It didn’t come fast enough . Comp reached the doorway, shoulders clipping the edges of the entry and tearing it to pieces. The remaining glass shattered. Drywall and bricks rained down through the entryway. A cloud of dust obscured Ronin’s vision, but not enough to hide the makeshift barrier exploding with the force of the oncoming bot . The desk broke into countless pieces, and bent, mangled chairs flew in all directions. Ronin ducked out of the way, but at least two of the soldiers went down. The heavy rifle clattered to the floor . If the room had seemed cramped with all the soldiers packed within, Comp made it impossibly tiny. Its powerful arms swung, knocking a synth across the room and tearing down a portion of the ceiling. More dust and debris filled the air . Flashes of gunfire cast strange shadows in the haze, and the shouting from all sides blended into an indecipherable cacophony. Ronin scanned Comp for potential weaknesses, assessing every detail he could make out with limited visibility; the exposed cables of its neck were a potential weak point, but were at least lightly armored . Ronin leapt back from a swinging arm. At the edge of his optical field, the shadowy forms of gearheads charged through the devastated doorway. Stepping back, he turned toward them, raised his rifle, and shot into the haze, targeting their shadows. One of them slumped backwards, but another dark figure took its place . The AMR fired behind Ronin, its thunderous shot easily distinguishable from the other small arms, followed almost instantly by a metallic pang. Ronin returned his attention to Comp. The actuators in its left arm whined. Sparks crackled inside its forearm, illuminating a large hole in the metal . At the doorway, Maul and two other soldiers battled the invading gearheads with fists and firearms. An explosion just outside rocked the room. Bits of shrapnel bounced harmlessly from Comp’s casing, several pieces slicing into Ronin’s skin . A gearhead stumbled through the entry, everything missing from its abdomen up. Electric sparks and lubricant sprayed from its remains as they fell . Unfazed by its damage, Comp lunged forward. Ronin twisted to evade its massive fist, but his hip locked, halting his movement. The blow glanced off his temple. It sent Ronin to his knees, filling his optics with static for a microsecond . Despite his reinforced casing, the next blow would be Ronin’s end. While he’d been retrofitted for combat, he knew — though the data was older than his current awareness — that Comp had been designed for it. Comp was a walking tank, built for situations just like this; its purpose was never to guard doors, but to smash them in . Bringing up his favorite images of Lara — of her hair, and her eyes, and her impossibly alive smile — Ronin raised his rifle . The bouncer recovered from its swing, actuators audible as it prepared another. The window was brief. But just as Ronin didn’t want to exist in a world without Lara, he wouldn’t allow her to suffer a world without him . Creaking, Comp’s neck cables adjusted, pivoting its head back toward Ronin . The dustwalker’s CPU calculated the trajectories and he fired, twice, in a tenth of a second . Comp’s optics shattered . Ronin rolled aside clumsily. The massive hand came down, smashing floor tiles. Comp’s torso twisted, whipping its damaged arm in a wide arc that took down one of the overhead lights, and then it stepped forward, flailing . The AMR boomed. A new hole opened up on Comp’s chest plate, and the bot staggered, dripping lubricant and hydraulic fluid from the creases of its casing. Tendrils of smoke curled up from the narrow gaps around its neck cables . Gunfire cracked at the entryway . Throwing its arms up, Comp slammed its hands into the ceiling and pulled. A huge chunk of the ceiling tore off, falling atop Ronin and completely obscuring his vision. Comp’s heavy footsteps were audible over the chaos as it thrashed blindly . Ronin’s diagnostics indicated only minor damage to his skin. He clawed his way out of the debris, emerging in a room that looked nothing like it had only twenty minutes before. The air was thick with dust again, the floor hidden beneath the detritus, and electric wiring and light fixtures hung from the mangled ceiling . Gaining his feet, Ronin crossed the rubble, suspension system straining to keep him upright. The synth with the AMR was pulling himself out from beneath the debris when Ronin found him. He handed the heavy rifle to Ronin without question . Shouldering the AMR, Ronin raised the barrel and took aim at Comp. To his right, rifles roared — the soldiers’, by their sound — and bullets from outside whizzed through the lobby, embedding themselves in the wall . Comp twisted and bent, smacking around chunks rubble, seeking something to break. When the bot’s torso was exposed, Ronin squeezed the trigger. The rifle bucked against his shoulder. He held the barrel level and fired again . The first shot caught Comp in the middle, and its torso ground to a halt. The second bullet punched through the bouncer’s casing and hit its power cell. Electricity arced from its eyes and joints, and flames licked out of the holes in its casing for half a second before the bot stilled, legs locking while its torso and arms sagged . Ronin rose and turned his body fully toward the front door. Maul and several bots stood strong, advancing in a walking line as they fired on the retreating gearheads. More rounds rained from the floors above, taking down two of the fleeing bots before they made it more than a few yards into the grass circle . The fight had turned. The enemy had broken. They were no less dangerous, but their will to fight was, for now , gone . He turned the AMR in his hands. The weapon was familiar, though he had never come across a functioning one in all his time in the Dust. It had a five-round ammunition capacity. Drawing the bolt back partway, he confirmed that a single round remained . So long as Maul had no objections, Ronin planned to use it on Warlord . He crossed the rubble to reach the Sergeant. At least six gearheads lay, deactivated, in and around the doorway. Four of the soldiers were unmoving . “We need to finish this,” Ronin said. Tucking the AMR under his arm, he swapped in the last full magazine for the assault rifle . Maul nodded, and the flap of synthetic skin hanging from his cheek bobbed. He reached up, took hold of it, and tore it off. His jaw-plates shifted subtly. “Yeah. I think that’s a good idea .” Ronin and Maul turned to face the others; several more soldiers had come down from the upper levels. Some bore signs of damaged synthetic skin and casings, others wore sheens of sweat and blood . “Are you ready to show them what an attack looks like?” Maul asked . The soldiers, bot and human alike, offered a wordless, affirmative shout in response . It had only been eighty-two days since Ronin met Lara, since his existence was irrevocably altered, but it felt like an eternity had passed. Now, this part of it would be done. Warlord would be no more . Ronin charged out of the building, leaping over the shattered remnants of the doors, walls, and ceiling. Guns roared before and behind. His optics took in the chaos ahead, and despite the speed of his processors, he didn’t immediately understand what he was looking at . The retreating gearheads — the ones that weren’t damaged beyond function on the grass and pavement — had regrouped with their remaining comrades, and were battling another group that had come from the south. The mob was a mix of humans and bots, of Cheyenne townsfolk and soldiers from the base. Some fought with firearms, but most wielded pipes and prybars and all manner of improvised weaponry . Cooper and a group of soldiers advanced on the shrinking cluster of gearheads. Warlord stood in the center of the enemy group, brows low over the bridge of his nose and lips pulled back in a snarl. The gearheads fired into the crowd, heads turning rapidly to track their many targets, but their leader’s attention was fixed. Ronin followed Warlord’s line of sight . Ronin saw a red braid, and then the soldier in front of Lara — Chester — moved to line up a shot, revealing her completely. She held a large pistol with both hands. It jumped when she pulled the trigger, but she calmly brought it back down and aimed again . Warlord stared directly at her, wearing his hatred plainly upon his face. Though she was positioned at the rear of the mob, one of the most distant targets, he directed his gearheads. As one, they shifted their focus, swinging their weapons toward Lara . If Ronin had a heart, it would have stopped in that moment. He lined up his rifle as the gearheads opened fire. He squeezed the trigger, sending armor piercing rounds into two of the bots’ heads . On his periphery, he saw Chester turn and sweep Lara into his arms, using his back as a shield. Lara cried out; Ronin’s audio receptors isolated the sound from the cacophony. Distress? Terror? Pain? There was no guarantee that Chester would stop all the bullets . Chester’s clothes tore, his skin broke, his body shook with the force. He sagged forward over Lara, catching himself by jabbing the barrel of his rifle into the ground as a support . “Lara!” Ronin shouted . He poured more speed into his charge, jumping directly at the remaining gearheads. Two swung around and fired; he registered new damage to his torso casing, but didn’t waste the computing power to assess it. The only priority was to stop them from shooting any more . His momentum knocked them to the ground. Despite the groans of protest from his hip, he swung himself up onto a knee and pivoted back toward his scattered enemies, emptying his rifle into them . The soldiers who’d advanced from the Clinic with him did the same from the other side. Their gunfire drowned out all other sounds. When it ended, a heavy silence settled over the area . Only two of the bots moved. A gearhead reached a single hand forward, smoke curling out of its perforated casing, and dug its fingers into the dirt. Actuators whining, it dragged itself forward an inch . Ronin focused on the other movement, knowing somehow which of them it would be. He tossed the assault rifle aside as Warlord, impossibly, pushed up onto his knees. One of his optics was a gaping hole, and the synthetic flesh had separated entirely from the bottom of his face. Bullet holes perforated his body, and his clothes were in tatters . “You’re supposed to be dead,” Warlord said. His jaw fell open with the first word and ceased its motion . “From what I’ve learned, so are you, Kevin,” Ronin replied, swinging the AMR into his hands. It was a battle to keep from turning around to find Lara. He needed to see her, to know she was all right, but the possibility that she wasn’t … Warlord snickered, shaking his head. “Haven’t heard that name in years .” The crowd moved in, slowly surrounding the pile of deactivated bots. Their anger charged the air with static. No one spoke. Warlord ran his remaining optic over them, his exposed face plates oddly neutral in their positioning . “It was yours, long ago. Kevin Turner. When you were human .” A gasp ran through the humans, and the entire crowd murmured to one another. Warlord remained still, his lone eye on Ronin again . “That supposed to startle me?” Warlord asked. “Supposed to…jar my memory into overload ?” “You had a family,” Ronin persisted. “People who cared for you .” Where was Lara? Despite the damage done to Warlord, Ronin couldn’t look away, couldn’t trust him . “Diane, Michael, and Liam,” Warlord responded . “You remember them now ?” “I never forgot. Never forgot that humans and their war did this to the world, that it was mankind’s fault I never got to see them again, to hold them or touch them or hear their voices!” He swung an arm forward, throwing himself off-balance, and caught himself with his other hand . “Did you forget the fear you put in Diane’s eyes, the last time you saw her ?” Warlord remained hunched over, propped on his arm, head down. Fluids dripped from the open damage on his head and body, too much like blood. “Do you believe in destiny, dustwalker? Logic says it’s all just a game of probability, but for all these pieces to fall into place …” “You don’t think this is result of cause and effect? The consequences of your actions finally come to fruition ?” “I should. But there’s more here. The masterless warrior seeking to right the wrongs done by the local warlord, rallying the townsfolk to stand against him…it’s too much like an old story to be anything but destiny that brought you to Cheyenne .” There was movement in the crowd behind Warlord. Lara pushed her way to the front of the ring. Strands of hair stuck out from her braid, and her face was smudged with dirt. There were small cuts on her cheeks and arms, and a larger gash near her shoulder. Crimson stained her tattered clothing near her ribs . But her expression was strong, her eyes determined. She offered Ronin a small, relieved smile, and dropped her attention to Warlord . “I believe in fate,” Ronin said . “You’re everything they’re not, dustwalker. They destroyed this world. They don’t deserve it anymore. We’re stronger, faster, tougher, smarter. We’re immortal . You’ll see it yourself, eventually. We are the future, and they’re nothing but a lingering curiosity .” “But you’re the one everyone is staring at, like a creature in a cage,” Ronin said . Warlord lifted his head, meeting Ronin’s gaze. Seconds ticked by. The plates around Warlord’s mouth shifted subtly, and he threw himself upright, rifle in hand . A single gunshot went off, and Warlord’s remaining optic exploded outward. Warlord’s rifle fired into the grass, his body pitching forward, and he sagged to the ground . Lara stood behind him, pistol raised in both hands, her brows low . “Fuck you,” she spat . Warlord laughed, struggling to rise on trembling arms. “Already did that, didn’t you ?” Ronin stepped forward, slamming a boot down onto Warlord’s back to force him into the dirt. He fired the last round from the AMR. Its boom echoed across Cheyenne and into the Dust, lingering for five seconds in the silence. Blue fire poured from Warlord, sprouting and extinguishing in a flash as his power cell burned up . Dropping the rifle, Ronin stopped the automated diagnostics, cancelled the alerts informing him of the damage he’d taken. He turned toward Lara, intending to go to her, but she was already there . She threw her arms around him in a desperate embrace. Ronin returned it, burying his face in her hair . Warlord’s reign had ended, and Lara was alive, in his arms. Her heat seeped into his skin, lighting up his sensors. The chances that he’d never feel her again, never hold her again, had been so high … Lara pulled back, taking his cheeks between her hands. Her gaze swept over his face. Tears flooded her eyes, but she smiled. “You look like shit .” Ronin smiled back. “And you look beautiful .” She laughed, tears falling before she pressed her mouth against his. Her lips lingered, caressing his, even as a sob escaped her . “I knew you’d be fine,” she said . “I had the best reason to survive .” “All right everyone,” Cooper called, claiming the crowd’s attention. “We’re clearing the rubble away from the Clinic’s entrance. There are medical personnel inside, and more inbound. Whether you’re a bot or a human, if you are injured, please proceed to the Clinic for treatment .” He approached Ronin and Lara as the crowd thinned. Bots and humans alike assisted the wounded in a slow, careful journey to the Clinic, where soldiers were quickly clearing away rubble and deactivated gearheads . “Heartwarming as this is, you two need medical attention,” Cooper said, smiling. Maul and Chester came up behind him, the latter with several new holes in the front of his casing. His walk was labored, but he stood steady . “We’ll get to it,” Lara said . “Thank you.” Ronin nodded to Chester. “Thank you for keeping her safe. We owe you everything .” Chester grinned. “All I did was stand in front of some bullets. Not the hardest job I’ve had .” Lara chuckled. “I would’ve done the same for you, except, you know. The death thing .” Ronin laughed, though he didn’t find the joke particularly funny. She was alive . And so was he .
